title: 12 Tahsas — St. Constantine & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "12 Tahsas — St. Constantine & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"12 Tahsas — St. Constantine & The Assembly

narrative\": \"The twelfth day of Tahsas honors Saint Constantine the Great, the Emperor who in the year three hundred and thirteen issued the Edict of Milan granting freedom of worship to the Christians of the Roman Empire, and who for the remainder of his reign labored to establish the Church in the full dignity of her public life. Constantine's name in its Latin form means 'steadfast' or 'constant,' and whatever the complexities of his theological understanding and his personal conduct, his steadfastness in the protection of the Church proved the hinge upon which the history of Christian civilization turned. The Ethiopian Church, inheritor of the Alexandrian tradition that flourished under his patronage, venerates him alongside his mother Helena as 'equal to the apostles' — a title that acknowledges his role in making possible the public proclamation of the Gospel throughout the civilized world.

Saint Spiridon of Trimythus, the wonder-working bishop of Cyprus whose feast falls on this day, was a simple shepherd who became a bishop and attended the Council of Nicaea where he defended the divinity of Christ with such directness and such accompanying miracles that even the most sophisticated of the assembled philosophers were silenced. His name in the Greek tradition has been connected with the word speira, meaning a coil or winding — perhaps an ancient reference to basket-weaving, for Spiridon continued to make baskets even after his elevation to the episcopate, giving the proceeds to the poor. He is among the most beloved saints of the Orthodox world, and the incorrupt body which rests to this day in the cathedral of Corfu is venerated by pilgrims from every land.

Saint John the Almsgiver, Patriarch of Alexandria in the early seventh century, completes this day's assembly. John, whose name means 'the Lord is gracious,' practiced an almsgiving so extreme that his servants repeatedly complained that he had given away all the treasures of the patriarchal see; he replied that there was always more to give, for the Lord who had given His own Son would not leave His almsgiver destitute. John ransomed slaves, fed the hungry, clothed the naked, built hospitals, and organized the distribution of food to refugees from the Persian invasion of Palestine — all while maintaining a rigorous personal ascesis and a pastoral attention to the theological formation of his clergy.

The theological seal of this twelfth day of Tahsas is the mystery of royal generosity. Constantine opens the empire to the Gospel; Spiridon opens the simplicity of his shepherd's wisdom to confound the proud; John the Almsgiver opens the storehouses of the Church to the poor. All three in their distinct vocations reflect the generosity of the God who in the Incarnation gave not a gift but the Giver Himself — who withheld not His own Son but delivered Him up for us all, the supreme act of almsgiving in the history of the universe.
